description: Slip and fall accidents caused by wet floors, uneven surfaces, poor lighting, or unmarked hazards throughout the terminal.
frequency_note: Leading cause of airport injury claims — hundreds occur daily across US airports
danger_note: Hip fractures, spinal injuries, and head trauma common — especially dangerous for elderly passengers

venue_specific_hazards.coffee_shops: Spilled beverages on smooth tile floors near condiment stations
venue_specific_hazards.restaurants: Grease tracked from kitchen areas into dining zones
venue_specific_hazards.restrooms: Wet floors from cleaning or sink splashback without adequate signage
venue_specific_hazards.moving_walkways: Transition points where walkway meets stationary floor
venue_specific_hazards.jet_bridges: Rain water tracked in from outdoor boarding in wet weather

liability_notes: Liability splits based on who controlled the specific zone. Concessionaires responsible for their lease areas; airport authority for common areas.
common_injuries: Hip fractures — especially in elderly passengers
Wrist fractures from catching the fall
Knee injuries including ACL and meniscus tears
Spinal injuries — herniated discs, compression fractures
Traumatic brain injury from head impact
Soft tissue injuries — contusions and lacerations
typical_recovery_time: 6 weeks to 18 months depending on injury
key_evidence: Surveillance footage — overwritten in 72 hours, preserve immediately
Cleaning and inspection logs for the zone where the fall occurred
Incident report filed at the airport — get the report number
Prior incident reports from the same location
Photographs of the hazard, scene, and footwear taken at the time
Medical records from same-day treatment
Witness statements from nearby passengers
Weather and foot traffic logs if weather-related fall
evidence_notes: CCTV footage is typically overwritten within 24-72 hours. Send a preservation demand letter to airport operations immediately.
liable_party_defenses: Comparative fault — you were distracted by your phone
Warning signs were visible and adequate
The hazard was open and obvious
You were wearing inappropriate footwear
We had no notice of the hazard — it was transient
The cleaning schedule was followed correctly
You failed to watch where you were walking
The fall was caused by a pre-existing medical condition
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Prove inspection logs show the area was not checked in hours before the fall
Show CCTV footage demonstrates the hazard existed long before the incident
Demonstrate warning signs were absent, inadequate, or placed after the fall
Show prior incident reports from the same zone establish constructive notice
Use expert testimony that the footwear was reasonable for airport travel
Prove the hazard was not open and obvious given the lighting and foot traffic
statute_of_limitations_note: 1-3 years depending on state. Government-operated airports may require Notice of Claim within 60-180 days.
compensable_damages: Future lost earning capacity if disability results
Pain and suffering — physical and emotional
Home modification costs if mobility is affected
Future medical treatment for long-term complications
factors_increasing_value: Prior slip incidents documented at the same location
Elderly victim with more severe injury outcome
CCTV showing the hazard existed before arrival of victim
No wet floor sign present despite known wet condition
Multiple defendants — operator and concessionaire both liable
Permanent disability or ongoing medical treatment required
airport_size_note: Occurs at every airport from Hartsfield-Jackson to Lewistown Municipal — larger airports have more incidents but smaller airports have fewer cleaning staff to respond
passenger_volume_note: Risk spikes dramatically during peak hours — a floor hazard at LAX affects 10x more people per hour than a regional airport
cleaning_contractor_types: ABM Industries (largest US airport facilities contractor)
contributing_environmental_factors: Snow and ice dragged through entrances
reportability: Often reported to airport operations — incident report number is critical
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: 200-500+ reported incidents per year at airports like ATL, LAX, ORD
red_flags_for_attorneys: No wet floor sign present
Incident happened during regular cleaning hours
Recurring complaints at same location
Elderly victim with hip fracture

description: Accidents involving airport ground vehicles including baggage carts, tugs, electric carts, and personal vehicles in curbside and roadway areas.
frequency_note: Dozens of incidents reported weekly at major airports; many go unreported as minor
danger_note: Heavy baggage tugs and carts can cause severe crush injuries and broken bones on impact

venue_specific_hazards.curbside: High-speed baggage carts operated by untrained staff near passenger pickup
venue_specific_hazards.tarmac: Heavy tugs with poor turning radius near unsecured pedestrian zones
venue_specific_hazards.parking_lots: Shuttle buses making sharp turns in tight lots with poor pedestrian visibility
venue_specific_hazards.baggage_ramps: Vehicles reversing without audible warnings or spotters

primary_liable_parties: Ground handling company (employer of cart operator)
Airline (if using their own ground crew)
liability_notes: Ground handlers like Menzies, Swissport, and dnata carry their own liability policies. Airlines may be vicariously liable for contracted ground crews.
common_injuries: Leg and ankle fractures from direct vehicle impact
Pelvic fractures — high severity, long recovery
Crush injuries to feet and lower extremities
Spinal injuries from the force of impact
Traumatic brain injury if victim fell after impact
Soft tissue damage — bruising, lacerations, torn muscles
Shoulder and arm fractures from defensive fall
key_evidence: Vehicle telematics and GPS data — 30-90 day retention window
Dashcam footage from the vehicle if equipped
CCTV from the incident area — 72-hour deletion window
Vehicle maintenance records and inspection logs
Operator training and certification records
Incident report from ground handler and airport authority
Witness statements from other passengers and airport staff
Medical records from same-day treatment
evidence_notes: GPS telematics data from ground vehicles is often overwritten within days. Demand preservation of all vehicle data immediately.
liable_party_defenses: The passenger stepped into a restricted vehicle area
The vehicle was operating within posted speed limits
The driver had right of way in the vehicle corridor
Contributory negligence — victim was not paying attention
The vehicle had all required safety signals active
Incident occurred in an area clearly marked for vehicle use
The ground handler followed all standard operating procedures
Weather conditions were a contributing factor beyond our control
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Vehicle telematics data shows speed or path was unsafe
CCTV demonstrates inadequate pedestrian separation in the area
Operator training records reveal insufficient safety certification
Prior incidents in the same zone establish pattern of negligence
Show vehicle was not equipped with required warning signals
Prove the area was de facto pedestrian space despite vehicle markings
statute_of_limitations_note: 2-3 years in most states. Government airport authority claims may require 60-180 day notice.
compensable_damages: Lost wages — including long-term if disability results
Vehicle damage if personal property was involved
Future medical costs for permanent injuries
factors_increasing_value: Telematics data showing excessive speed
Prior incidents involving the same operator or vehicle type
Pedestrian area that was inadequately separated from vehicle corridor
Multiple defendants — ground handler and airport authority
Serious or permanent injury to lower extremities
CCTV showing no warning given before vehicle entered area
airport_size_note: Primarily occurs at large hubs with heavy ground vehicle traffic — ramp areas at major carriers are extremely high-traffic
passenger_volume_note: Baggage handling volume directly correlates with cart and vehicle traffic — peak baggage hours create the highest risk
contributing_environmental_factors: Inadequate vehicle traffic separation from pedestrians
reportability: Must be reported to airport security and police — creates a formal incident record
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: 50-200+ incidents per year at major hubs
red_flags_for_attorneys: Driver was unlicensed or untrained
Vehicle had no functioning backup alarm
Incident in unmarked pedestrian zone
Operator was speeding or distracted

danger_note: Falls from jet bridge gaps can result in severe injury or death
high_risk_times: Tight turnaround times with rushed docking

venue_specific_hazards.jet_bridge_floor: Worn non-slip surfaces losing grip in wet conditions
venue_specific_hazards.jet_bridge_mechanism: Sudden mechanical stop of bridge height adjustment during boarding
venue_specific_hazards.gate_area: Crowd surge during boarding causing falls near jet bridge entrance

Surveillance footage of boarding process
Incident report filed with airline
FAA safety reporting records
Incident report filed at the airport — obtain the reference number before leaving
Witness statements gathered at the scene before departure

liable_party_defenses: Passenger was not paying attention
Bridge was within acceptable tolerances
Adequate lighting and markings were present
Comparative fault of the victim contributed to the incident
All applicable safety standards were met at the time of the incident
No prior similar incidents at this location establish constructive notice
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Obtain maintenance history showing deferred repairs
Show prior similar incidents at same gate
Expert testimony on industry standards for bridge gaps
Maintenance and inspection logs establish inadequate pre-incident safety checks
Prior incident records at the same location establish constructive notice
statute_of_limitations_note: 2-3 years for premises claims. Airline-related claims may be subject to federal aviation law.
factors_increasing_value: Defective equipment with prior complaints
Elderly or disabled victim
Permanent disability
Airline aware of hazard
airport_size_note: Jet bridges exist at all hub airports and most commercial terminals — regional airports without jet bridges shift risk to boarding stairs
passenger_volume_note: Faster turnarounds during peak hours mean less time for proper jet bridge positioning — increases gap risk
contributing_environmental_factors: Rain during outdoor jet bridge sections
victim_demographics: Passengers looking at phones during boarding
reportability: Report immediately to gate agent — airline incident report number is essential
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: 20-100+ incidents per year at large hub airports
red_flags_for_attorneys: Gap between bridge and aircraft threshold exceeds 1 inch
Prior maintenance complaints on record
No lighting over threshold area
Bridge was misaligned at time of incident

description: Injuries in baggage claim areas including falling luggage from carousels, conveyor belt malfunctions, overcrowding injuries, and trip hazards.
frequency_note: One of the most congested areas in any airport — incidents occur multiple times daily at busy airports
danger_note: Heavy suitcases falling from carousels can cause serious foot, ankle, and head injuries

venue_specific_hazards.carousels: Overloaded carousels ejecting bags onto crowded floor areas
venue_specific_hazards.oversized_claim: Heavy sports equipment, strollers, and bikes creating floor obstructions
venue_specific_hazards.carousel_edge: Passengers leaning dangerously over moving belt edges
venue_specific_hazards.exit_corridors: Congested narrow exits with wheeled luggage creating trip hazards

liability_notes: Airport authority typically owns and maintains carousels. Handling contractors operate them. Both can be named as defendants.
common_injuries: Back injuries from heavy bag impact or awkward lifting
Shoulder injuries — rotator cuff damage from falling bags
Head injuries if struck by falling luggage
Wrist and arm fractures from defensive reactions
Knee injuries from crowd pressure and falls
Foot crush injuries from bags falling from carousels
Lacerations from broken baggage or metal components
key_evidence: CCTV of the carousel area — 72-hour deletion window
Baggage loading manifests and weight records
Carousel maintenance and inspection logs

Photographs of the scene and any visible hazards
Medical records from same-day treatment
Witness statements from other passengers at the carousel
Airline baggage handling procedures documentation
evidence_notes: Report immediately to the baggage services desk to create a paper trail. Request incident report number before leaving.
liable_party_defenses: The bag weight was within airline-permitted limits
The carousel was operating within normal parameters
The passenger reached for a bag in an unsafe manner

plaintiff_counter_strategies: Demonstrate carousel area was inadequately managed during peak volume
Show prior baggage claim incidents at the same carousel
Prove the bag was loaded improperly creating a foreseeable hazard

Show the airline failed to apply required weight distribution protocols

compensable_damages: Future medical costs for chronic injuries
Personal property damage if additional luggage was damaged
factors_increasing_value: CCTV showing the carousel was overcrowded before the incident
Prior baggage claim incidents at the same carousel on record
Improperly loaded bags with documented weight violations
Serious injury requiring surgery
Multiple defendants — airline and airport operator both liable
Evidence of inadequate baggage area staffing during peak hours

passenger_volume_note: International arrival waves create extremely dangerous overcrowding at carousels — multiple heavy bags simultaneously on carousel increases ejection risk

contributing_environmental_factors: Bags piled on floor creating obstacles
victim_demographics: Passengers tripping on other people's luggage

average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: 100-300 incidents per year at major international gateways

description: Injuries at TSA security checkpoints including falls from rushing, equipment injuries, overcrowding incidents, and improper screening procedures.
frequency_note: TSA screens over 2 million passengers daily — checkpoint injuries are a regular occurrence at all major airports
danger_note: Checkpoint congestion creates dangerous fall conditions, especially during peak times when multiple lanes close unexpectedly
high_risk_times: When TSA closes lanes creating bottlenecks

venue_specific_hazards.conveyor_belt: Rushing to place items on belt causing trips on belt edges
venue_specific_hazards.body_scanner: Step-up platform causing falls during entry/exit
venue_specific_hazards.checkpoint_exit: Crowded exit area with no-belt floor transition
venue_specific_hazards.queuing_area: Stanchion poles and crowded conditions causing falls

secondary_liable_parties: Private security contractors at some airports
liability_notes: Claims against TSA must be filed under the Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) — requires administrative filing BEFORE lawsuit. This is a critical procedural requirement.
insurance_types_involved: Premises liability for airport authority portions
common_injuries: Shoulder injuries from aggressive arm manipulation
Wrist and hand injuries from forced screening positions
Back injuries from physical pressure applied during screening
Head injuries from falls caused by TSA actions
Knee injuries from forced positioning or falls
Bruising and soft tissue injuries from excessive pat-downs
Psychological trauma — PTSD from aggressive screening
Hip injuries in elderly passengers from physical instability during screening
key_evidence: TSA incident report — request immediately after the incident
CCTV from the checkpoint area — overwritten within 72 hours
TSA agent shift logs and deployment records
TSA Standard Operating Procedures violated during the incident
Prior DHS complaints filed against the same TSA officer
Medical records from same-day treatment establishing causation
Witness statements from other passengers in the screening line
Your SF-95 administrative claim — must be filed within 2 years
evidence_notes: FTCA claims require filing an administrative claim with TSA/DHS within 2 years BEFORE you can file a lawsuit. Missing this step bars your claim permanently.
liable_party_defenses: TSA agent followed standard screening procedures
The passenger failed to comply with screening instructions
The force used was reasonable under the circumstances
Injury was caused by the passenger's own movement
TSA has discretionary function immunity for screening decisions
The passenger was warned before the screening action was taken
Pre-existing condition caused or contributed to the injury
TSA acted within its statutory authority and training guidelines
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Show TSA deviated from its own Standard Operating Procedures
Demonstrate force used exceeded what the situation required
Prove the agent was inadequately trained through training records
Show prior complaints against the same TSA officer or checkpoint
Medical evidence establishes injury was caused by screening action
CCTV from checkpoint area captures the sequence of events
legal_standard: Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) for TSA claims
statute_of_limitations_note: 2-year limit under FTCA — must file administrative claim first. Missing FTCA requirements permanently bars the claim.
compensable_damages: Future medical treatment for ongoing conditions
Travel costs if the incident caused a missed connection
Future lost earning capacity if disability results
Non-economic damages for civil rights violations where applicable
factors_increasing_value: Clear deviation from TSA Standard Operating Procedures
Prior complaints or disciplinary action against the agent
CCTV footage capturing the full incident sequence
Elderly or disabled victim with more severe injury outcome
Failure to provide medical assistance after injury occurred
Pattern of incidents at the same checkpoint
airport_size_note: Every commercial airport with TSA — all 522 US commercial airports have security checkpoints
passenger_volume_note: TSA checkpoint congestion is directly proportional to flight volume — shoulder seasons with reduced staff + high passengers = highest risk
contributing_environmental_factors: Rush to make flights creating hurry
Bins creating trip hazards at exit
Passengers removing shoes on slippery floors
victim_demographics: Passengers with mobility issues forced to remove shoes

insurance_claim_type: Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) — different from standard claims
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: 50-200+ TSA-related injury claims filed per year nationally

Inadequate matting at scanner exit

Adequate security staffing was in place at the time
The assailant's conduct was not predictable
Security cameras were operational and being monitored
The property was reasonably safe for its intended use
The victim assumed the risk of traveling in a public space
Prior security measures were adequate given the circumstances
The assault occurred in an area beyond our security responsibility

Show the pattern of prior incidents made the assault foreseeable

liability_notes: Worker claims involve two tracks: (1) workers compensation from employer, and (2) third-party liability claims against non-employer negligent parties.
common_injuries: Crush injuries from equipment and vehicles
Fractures from falls on ramp and tarmac surfaces
Repetitive stress injuries from baggage handling
Back injuries from heavy lifting operations
Chemical exposure injuries from de-icing and fuel handling
Hearing damage from extended jet engine exposure
Shoulder tears from overhead baggage handling
key_evidence: Workers compensation claim filed with direct employer
OSHA incident report and any OSHA citation records
Equipment maintenance and inspection records
Third-party contractor site access logs
CCTV of the incident area — 72-hour deletion window
Witness statements from co-workers and supervisors
Safety training records for all involved workers
Contract documents identifying which party owned the equipment or area
evidence_notes: Workers compensation has strict reporting deadlines (often 30 days). Third-party claims must be identified separately from workers comp claims.
liable_party_defenses: Workers compensation is the exclusive remedy against the direct employer
The worker assumed the risk inherent in their job duties
The injury was caused by the worker's own negligence
All OSHA safety requirements were met at the time of injury
The defective equipment was the worker's employer's responsibility
The hazard was created by the worker's own actions
Adequate safety training was provided before the injury
Third-party immunity applies under the workers comp scheme
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Identify all non-employer third parties who contributed to the injury
Show the airport authority failed to maintain safe common areas
Prove defective equipment was owned and maintained by a third party
Demonstrate OSHA violations by a contractor other than the employer
Show the unsafe condition was created by a different contractor's work
Third-party tort claims are not barred by workers comp exclusivity
statute_of_limitations_note: Workers comp reporting: often within 30 days. Third-party tort claims: 2-3 years.
compensable_damages: Medical treatment costs beyond workers comp limits
Full lost wages — not just workers comp partial replacement
Pain and suffering — not recoverable through workers comp
Future medical costs for permanent injuries
Punitive damages if willful OSHA violations are proven

Defective equipment owned by airport authority or different contractor

description: Physical injuries and significant harm arising from extended airline delays, tarmac groundings, flight cancellations, and forced overnight stays in airports.
frequency_note: Millions of passengers are affected by delays annually — physical harm claims are less common but legally significant
danger_note: Tarmac delays without adequate water, medical attention, or restroom access create documented physical harm
secondary_airport_zones: Terminal seating areas during extended waits
high_risk_times: During weather events causing system-wide delays

venue_specific_hazards.tarmac_grounding: No water, restrooms, or medical access during extended grounding
venue_specific_hazards.medication_deprivation: Stranded passenger unable to access checked medication
venue_specific_hazards.physical_collapse: Elderly or ill passenger collapsing from extended standing in rebooking lines
venue_specific_hazards.overnight_terminal: Passengers sleeping on hard floors causing exacerbation of existing conditions
venue_specific_hazards.rushing_injuries: Injuries from running to catch rebooked flight in unfamiliar airport

secondary_liable_parties: Air traffic control (FAA — FTCA if delay was ATC-caused)
liability_notes: DOT's 3-hour domestic / 4-hour international tarmac delay rule creates a regulatory baseline. Physical harm beyond financial inconvenience opens tort liability.
common_injuries: Aggravation of existing medical conditions from extended terminal waiting
Medication running out during unexpected extended delays
Falls and physical injuries in crowded delay areas
Medical emergencies worsened by inadequate airport response during delay
Missed medical appointments or treatments due to cancellation
Business losses from missed critical meetings or events
Dehydration and physical decline in elderly passengers during extended delays
key_evidence: Flight records showing actual vs scheduled departure times
Weather records for the delay period — establishes or refutes force majeure
Airline maintenance logs if mechanical issues caused the delay
DOT delay reporting data for the specific flight
Communications from airline to passenger during delay period
Medical records if a health incident occurred during the delay
Receipts for food, accommodation, and transportation incurred during delay
Documentation of missed connections or consequential trip losses
evidence_notes: Keep all receipts from delay-related expenses. File a DOT complaint immediately — creates official record.
liable_party_defenses: The delay or cancellation was caused by weather — a force majeure event
Air traffic control delays are outside the airline's control
The injury occurred during a period of extraordinary circumstances
Compensation offered meets all regulatory requirements
The passenger was informed of their rights under DOT regulations
The airline met all duty of care obligations during the delay
The medical emergency during the delay was not caused by airline negligence
The passenger declined offered accommodations during the delay
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Distinguish weather delays from maintenance delays — only weather is force majeure
Show the airline failed to provide required accommodations during extended delays
Prove inadequate medical response during a medical emergency mid-delay
Document the failure to communicate accurate delay information
Show the delay caused foreseeable physical harm beyond mere inconvenience
Prove the cancellation was caused by foreseeable staffing or mechanical issues
legal_standard: Negligence / DOT regulations / breach of contract
statute_of_limitations_note: 2-3 years for personal injury. Contract claims may have different periods.
compensable_damages: Out-of-pocket costs incurred during delay — food, accommodation, transport
Consequential business losses from missed meetings or events
Medical treatment costs if health incident occurred during delay
Future medical costs if delay aggravated a pre-existing condition
Trip cancellation losses if the journey could not be completed
Lost wages from delayed return to work
Pain and suffering for physical harm caused by delay conditions
factors_increasing_value: Mechanical or staffing delay misrepresented as weather delay
Medical emergency during delay with inadequate airport response
Elderly or disabled passenger with documented health consequences
Failure to provide DOT-required accommodations during extended delay
Pattern of delays by same airline on same route
Missed critical medical treatment as a result of cancellation
airport_size_note: Delay injuries occur at all airports but tarmac grounding incidents (worst category) primarily at large hubs with complex operations
passenger_volume_note: High-volume hub airports are most prone to cascading delays — a single weather event at ORD, ATL, or DFW can strand thousands simultaneously
cleaning_contractor_types: Not directly relevant — airline conduct is the issue
contributing_environmental_factors: Cascading delays from crew repositioning failures
victim_demographics: Diabetic/cardiac patients needing access to medication
reportability: File DOT complaint immediately (creates official timeline). Keep ALL receipts. Seek medical care same day if physical harm occurred
insurance_claim_type: Negligence / DOT violation / breach of contract
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: Physical harm cases: 100-500+ per year during major weather events
red_flags_for_attorneys: Tarmac delay exceeded 3 hours domestic / 4 hours international
Passenger was denied water, restroom, or medical access
Medication was in checked bag stranded on aircraft
DOT complaint was filed and airline denied valid claim
